allusions.suspected_source: Xenophon, Memorabilia 2.1.21--34 (the choice of Heracles, attributed to Prodicus)
allusions.translator_note: Cicero's standard way of referring to the Choice of Heracles in Xenophon's Memorabilia. The story is treated more fully at De Officiis 1.118.

allusions.suspected_source: Cornelius Nepos, Epaminondas 9 (or a common Hellenistic source)
allusions.translator_note: The dying Epaminondas at Mantinea (362 BC); the spear-point and the rescued shield were a stock pathetic exemplum.
The flight of Themistocles from Athens and his reception by the Persian king is the type of the great-career-of-doubtful-fortune.
Alexander at the supposed tomb of Achilles at Sigeum: ``fortunate Achilles to have had such a herald of his glory.'' The classic Hellenistic statement of the dependence of fame on the praiser.

allusions.surface: Hector ille mihi Naevianus, qui non tantum 'laudari' se laetatur, sed addit etiam 'a laudato viro.'
allusions.suspected_source: Cn. Naevius, Hector profiscens (Ribbeck ROL fr. 17)
allusions.translator_note: The Naevian fragment laetus sum laudari me abs te, pater, a laudato viro, addressed by Hector to Priam (or by Astyanax to Hector). A favourite tag of Cicero's, used here at climactic position; quoted again in Tusc. 4.67 and elsewhere.
